purpose: Classifies commodity sub-complexes by supply elasticity to determine which are candidates for structural bull markets versus managed or episodic price dynamics
components: Electrification metals (copper, lithium): supply elasticity ~0.05, multi-year structural tightness case, greenfield timelines 12-18 years
Hydrocarbons (oil, gas): supply elasticity ~0.5-1.0, OPEC+ managed $70-90 band, price ceiling enforced by spare capacity at 4-5Mb/d
Agriculture (wheat, corn, soy): supply elasticity ~0.5-0.7, episodic spikes mean-reverting within 12-24 months, biological production cycles
current_assessment: Trifurcation holds in steady state with total agent convergence (9/10). Only electrification metals have a multi-year structural case. Critical boundary condition: trifurcation fails during correlated demand shocks (March 19, 2026 evidence). 2/5 termination signals present = mid-cycle.
limitations: Supply-side framework that does not address demand-side re-correlation during macro shocks
Does not account for financial contagion channels (trading house leverage, CLO cascades) that correlate sub-complexes
Assumes OPEC+ band maintenance, which has ~7-10 year historical breakdown intervals
Aggregate +0.28 equity correlation may mask bimodal distributions along trifurcation lines

purpose: Models the self-limiting mechanism that terminates commodity supercycles through the inflation-rates-demand destruction feedback loop, with multiple complementary transmission channels
components: Price-to-inflation channel: commodity prices embed in headline inflation in 3-6 months
Inflation-to-rates channel: central bank tightening with novel dilemma for transition metals
Rates-to-demand channel: demand destruction operates with 12-18 month lag (~2x slower than inflation embedding)
Balance sheet channel: Kaleckian profit redistribution forces consuming-sector borrowing until credit impulse turns negative
CLO amplifier: higher rates compress CLO arbitrage, reducing issuance and tightening loan supply for commodity borrowers
Term premium channel: 5-10bp expansion per 10% copper move, independent of expected policy path
Treasury supply amplification: higher inflation raises debt service, forcing more issuance, widening term premium
current_assessment: All 8 agents confirmed across all 4 debate pairs. 4/4 historical supercycle terminations involved this mechanism. Timing asymmetry (inflation 2x faster than demand destruction) creates volatile transition periods. Open question: whether fiscal policy (IRA, EU Green Deal) can partially override demand destruction channel.
limitations: Timing asymmetry creates volatile transition period that is difficult to trade
Fiscal subsidies for transition metals may weaken rate-to-demand-destruction channel — never tested at this scale
Interaction between multiple channels is not quantified
Government-financed vs. private-financed transition produces fundamentally different fragility profiles

purpose: Monitors supercycle phase by tracking five historical termination signals to determine cycle positioning
components: Signal 1: Consensus recognition of supercycle — PRESENT (13/15 desks bullish, +2.1 sigma positioning)
Signal 2: Financial casualty evidence — PRESENT (Pimco -26% YTD, Conductor wound down, Millennium $1.5B loss)
Signal 3: Inventory rebuilding cycle begins — ABSENT
Signal 4: Major new supply sanctioning wave — ABSENT (permitting reform only 10-20% timeline compression)
Signal 5: Credit impulse turns negative — ABSENT (requires monitoring global private debt-to-GDP and China credit impulse)
current_assessment: 2/5 signals present indicates mid-cycle. The absence of inventory rebuilding and supply sanctioning are genuinely informative negative signals. Replaces the refuted consensus-to-peak timing estimate (2.5-5.0 years, n=4) as the preferred cycle positioning tool.
limitations: n=4 historical precedents with fundamentally different termination mechanisms
1970s and 2000s episodes (5-6 year lags) structurally different from war-driven episodes (1.5-2 years)
Novel features (energy transition fiscal support, post-GFC household deleveraging) may alter signal reliability
Signal weighting is qualitative, not calibrated

purpose: Classifies mining investment positions on Minsky's hedge-speculative-Ponzi spectrum to assess financial fragility of the copper supply pipeline
components: Hedge finance: brownfield operations with cash flow covering debt service at current prices (above AISC $8-9k/t)
Speculative finance: greenfield projects requiring refinancing during 12-18 year development but expected to generate returns at forecast prices
Ponzi finance: greenfield projects where copper falls below AISC during development, requiring new borrowing to service existing debt
Zombie zone ($8-11k/t): metastable state where brownfield survives but no new supply is sanctioned, creating structural tightness
current_assessment: Most greenfield copper projects constitute speculative finance. The 0/8 deficit forecast base rate means the required price trajectory has never been empirically validated. The zombie zone is the current metastable equilibrium breeding Minsky-style instability through deferred supply.
limitations: Single-agent origin (macro_strategist_02) though validated as 'genuinely original' by debate
Geological timescales (12-18 years) stretch Minsky's framework beyond its original credit-cycle context
Government subsidies and strategic stockpiling could alter the hedge/speculative boundary
Scrap supply growth may alter zombie zone boundaries

purpose: Uses asset class sequencing (which commodity leads the cycle) to classify whether the current episode is a monetary regime disruption or a demand-pull supercycle, with distinct investment implications
components: Monetary regime disruption pattern (1971-74): gold leads, metals follow, oil flat then spikes; structural opportunity begins AFTER regime stress resolves
Demand-pull pattern (2000s): oil and industrials lead, gold lags; broad commodity allocation is appropriate during the cycle
Falsification test: if oil and industrial metals break out together while gold consolidates, regime classification shifts to demand-pull
current_assessment: Current sequencing (gold $4,435 leading, copper following, oil flat) matches 1971-74 monetary disruption pattern. If correct, current copper positioning may be premature. Gold residual exceeding $2,000/oz supports monetary stress interpretation.
limitations: n=2 historical precedents — statistically insufficient for confident classification
Energy transition demand is a structural novelty with no historical analogue
Multiple competing explanations for gold outperformance cannot be distinguished without flow-of-funds decomposition
Does not account for possibility that both monetary and structural drivers operate simultaneously

purpose: Models inter-sectoral profit and credit flows during commodity supercycles to identify the balance sheet deterioration that precedes cycle termination
components: Kaleckian profit equation: Profits = Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports - Household Saving
Commodity supercycles redistribute profits from consuming to producing sectors
Consuming-sector firms borrow to maintain spending against rising commodity inputs
Government-financed transition (deficit spending) preserves private sector balances
Private-financed transition increases Minsky fragility in consuming sectors
Cycle terminates when credit impulse turns negative (borrowing capacity exhausted)
current_assessment: Validated for 2/4 historical terminations (1980, 2011); ambiguous for earlier episodes (1917, 1951). Current cycle has novel features: energy transition fiscal support may partially insulate private sector balances. Key data needed: global private debt-to-GDP ratios, China credit impulse.
limitations: 2/4 clear historical support, 2/4 ambiguous — does not meet rigorous empirical standards
Post-GFC household deleveraging may alter the consuming-sector borrowing channel
China's unique financial system may not conform to standard Kaleckian dynamics
Fiscal-monetary interaction not tested at current scale of transition subsidies

purpose: Identifies pricing inconsistencies across equity, credit, and futures surfaces for the same underlying commodity to reveal genuine uncertainty or mispricing
components: Equity-implied commodity price: reverse-engineer the price where mining equity valuations become consistent with other surfaces
Forward curve shape: backwardation/contango signals about physical tightness
Credit spread level: distress or complacency signals for commodity-sector borrowers
Inconsistency diagnosis: mutually contradictory pricing across surfaces implies at least one must reprice
current_assessment: Copper pricing surfaces are internally inconsistent. Equities imply $9.5-10k/t (moderate tightness), not the $12k+ required by the structural deficit thesis. Forward curve and credit spreads are both benign. The inconsistency reveals the market has not reached consensus on deficit magnitude.
limitations: Equity valuations reflect operating leverage, management quality, and jurisdiction risk beyond commodity price
Credit spreads may lag physical market developments due to covenant structures
Forward curve shape affected by financialization dynamics (contango bias from index rebalancing)
Surface reconciliation may require several quarters of data to resolve
